items.q: What is the rule of thirds?
items.correct: A guideline placing key elements along lines or intersections of a 3x3 grid.
items.misconception: A strict mathematical rule that must never be broken.
items.why: Students treat it as inviolable. It's a useful HEURISTIC for engaging compositions - many great works ignore it. Centred compositions can be powerful too; the rule is a starting point, not a law.

items.q: What is the difference between relief and intaglio printing?
items.correct: Relief prints from raised surfaces; intaglio prints from incised lines below the surface.
items.misconception: They are different names for the same process.
items.random_wrong: Relief is for paper, intaglio is for fabric.
items.why: Students don't grasp the inverse logic. Lino cuts AWAY non-printing areas (relief - raised inked); etching incises lines that HOLD ink below the surface (intaglio - ink pressed up into paper).

items.q: What can line in a composition imply, even without representing an object?
items.correct: Movement, direction, emotion, and visual rhythm.
items.misconception: Only the outline of an object.
items.why: Students treat line as merely descriptive (an outline). In design, line is expressive - jagged lines suggest energy, curves softness, diagonals movement, even abstractly.

items.q: Which technique uses crossing lines for tone?
items.why: Correct answer: Cross-hatching. “Stippling” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: Dots. “Lines” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many primary colours are commonly used in pigment mixing?
items.why: Exact answer: 3 colours. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
